SEARCH
Package sprouts

Class Guarded<V extends @Nullable Object>

java.lang.Object
sprouts.Guarded<V>
Type Parameters:
V - the type of the guarded value; strongly recommended to be immutable
public final class Guarded<V extends @Nullable Object> extends Object
A mutable container that owns its lock: the guarded value can only be read or written through methods that acquire the lock for you, so the lock can never be forgotten, leaked, or unbalanced.

Guarded is the blocking sibling of AtomicReference. Both wrap a single value and offer functional update methods such as updateAndGet; the difference is the concurrency strategy:

  • AtomicReference is lock-free. Its update function may be re-applied several times under contention (compare-and-swap retry loop), so that function must be cheap and side-effect-free.
  • Guarded takes a mutual-exclusion lock. Its update function runs exactly once, while every other writer waits. This is the right trade when the update is expensive (so you cannot afford to recompute it) or when you genuinely want updates serialized.

Inspired by Rust's Mutex<T>, where the lock owns the data rather than sitting beside it. Java cannot enforce that ownership at the type level, so the safety contract below is your responsibility instead of the compiler's.

Two ways to use it

1. Immutable-swap mode (recommended). V is an immutable type (a record, String, immutable collection, etc.). Each update produces a new value that replaces the old reference. Nothing the value exposes can be mutated, so even a reference handed out by get() stays safe. 


 record Account(String owner, long cents) {
     Account deposit(long c) { return new Account(owner, cents + c); }
 }

 Guarded<Account> account = Guarded.of(new Account("Ada", 0));

 // Runs exactly once under the lock, even if many threads call deposit concurrently:
 account.update(a -> a.deposit(500));

 long balance = account.read(Account::cents);

Sprouts' own immutable containers are ideal payloads for this mode: Tuple, Association and ValueSet all return a new instance on every "modification", so guarding one reduces to swapping the reference under the lock — no copying, no escape hazard. 


 Guarded<Tuple<Order>> orders = Guarded.of(Tuple.of(Order.class));

 orders.update(t -> t.add(newOrder)); // swap in a new tuple, under the lock
 int count = orders.read(Tuple::size); // read a snapshot, under the lock

2. Guarded-mutable mode. V is a mutable object (a HashMap, a builder, a primitive array...) that you never let escape. Touch it only via mutate(Consumer) and read(Function), and never store the reference returned by get(). 


 Guarded<Map<String, Integer>> counts = Guarded.of(new HashMap<>());

 counts.mutate(m -> m.merge("hits", 1, Integer::sum));   // safe: mutated under the lock
 int hits = counts.read(m -> m.getOrDefault("hits", 0)); // safe: read under the lock

Observing changes from another thread

A Guarded is written by many threads, but a Var/Val property is meant to be owned by one: its change listeners fire synchronously on whatever thread calls set(..). viewOn(Executor) bridges the two. It returns a read-only Viewable whose value tracks this container, with every update delivered through the Executor you supply — so the property and all of its listeners run on that executor's thread: 


 // 'ui' is a single-threaded executor — e.g. SwingUtilities::invokeLater wrapped as an Executor.
 Guarded<Account> account = Guarded.of(new Account("Ada", 0));
 Viewable<Account> view = account.viewOn(ui);

 view.onChange(From.ALL, it -> label.setText(it.currentValue().orElseThrow().toString())); // runs on 'ui'

 // ...on any background thread:
 account.update(a -> a.deposit(500)); // the view re-syncs on 'ui', firing the listener there

A single viewOn(Executor) method covers every case: the view inherits this container's type and null policy, so a nullable Guarded produces a null-permitting view (viewable even while it currently holds null) and a non-null one produces a non-null view. To turn a nullable container into a null-safe view, use viewOn(Executor, Object) with a fallback value that stands in whenever the value is null.

This is the Guarded analogue of observeOn(scheduler) in Rx-style libraries, or a conflated StateFlow in Kotlin. Three rules make it safe — and it is dangerous if you ignore them, because misuse fails silently:

  • The executor must be effectively single-threaded (a single-thread executor, a UI dispatch loop, an actor mailbox...). It is what gives the property a well-defined owner thread; a multi-threaded pool would fire the property concurrently and reintroduce the very races this class exists to prevent. The type system cannot enforce this — it is on you.
  • V must be immutable. The view hands the value to another thread, so a mutable V would escape the lock. This is exactly the immutable-swap mode above; do not combine viewOn with mutate(Consumer).
  • Delivery is conflated (latest-wins). If writes outpace the executor, intermediate values may be skipped; the view always converges to the most recent value rather than queuing every step. Treat it as a live view of current state, not an event log.

The view is held weakly: drop your reference to it and it (with its listeners) becomes eligible for collection and quietly stops receiving updates — no unsubscribe needed.

Safety contract

  • Do not let the value escape unguarded. If V is mutable, a reference returned by get() (or captured inside an updater) can be mutated outside the lock, which silently defeats all protection. Prefer immutable V; otherwise treat read(Function) / mutate(Consumer) as the only doors in.
  • Keep update/read/mutate functions self-contained. They run while the lock is held. Calling into another Guarded (or any other lock) from inside one reintroduces classic lock-ordering deadlock risk. Re-entering this same instance is permitted (the underlying lock is reentrant) but is usually a sign to restructure.
  • Mind the hold time. The lock is held for the entire duration of your function. That is exactly what you want for one expensive, serialized computation, but under heavy contention it serializes throughput. If many threads each need a long update, consider funnelling the work onto a single-threaded executor instead.

Memory visibility

Because every access goes through the lock, the lock's happens-before guarantees make writes visible to subsequent readers automatically. The guarded field therefore needs no volatile. This holds only as long as the "no unguarded access" rule above is respected.

Nullability

Exactly like Var, a Guarded chooses a null policy at construction and enforces it at runtime. A non-null container, created with of(Object), rejects every attempt to store null with a NullPointerException and is therefore guaranteed to never hold null. A nullable container, created with ofNullable(Class, Object) or ofNull(Class), permits null. The container also knows its own type, again like Var, and enforces it: any value whose class is not assignable to type() is rejected with an IllegalArgumentException. Query the policy with allowsNull().

This class is thread-safe. It is intentionally not Serializable and does not override equals/hashCode (it has mutable identity, like AtomicReference).

See Also:
  • AtomicReference
  • Tuple
  • Association
  • ValueSet

    V
    accumulateAndGet(V x, BinaryOperator<V> accumulator)
    Combines the current value with x using accumulator and stores the result, returning the new value.

    Returns whether this container permits null, like Val.allowsNull().

    Sets the value to newValue if the current value equals expectedValue.

    Replaces the value and returns the value that was present beforehand.

    Applies updater to the current value, stores the result, and returns the previous value.

    Returns whether any thread currently holds the lock.

    Runs mutator against the current value under the lock, without swapping the reference.

    Creates a non-null Guarded holding item, with its type inferred from the item's runtime class.

    Like of(Object), but lets you choose the lock's fairness policy.

    Creates a nullable Guarded of the given type, holding item (which may be null).

    Like ofNullable(Class, Object), but lets you choose the lock's fairness policy.

    Returns a string form of the current value, read under the lock.

    Returns a read-only Viewable property that mirrors this container's value, with every change delivered through executor.
    Viewable<V>
    viewOn(Executor executor, V fallback)
    Like viewOn(Executor), but the resulting view never publishes null: whenever this container holds null, the view shows fallback instead.

  • Method Details

    • of

      public static <V> Guarded<V> of(V item)
      Creates a non-null Guarded holding item, with its type inferred from the item's runtime class. Like Var.of(Object), the resulting container is guaranteed to never hold null: every attempt to store null is rejected at runtime. 
      
 Guarded<Account> account = Guarded.of(new Account("Ada", 0));
      Type Parameters:
      V - the value type
      Parameters:
      item - the initial value; must not be null
      Returns:
      a new non-null Guarded
      Throws:
      NullPointerException - if item is null
      See Also:
      • of(Class, Object)
      • ofNullable(Class, Object)

    • of

      public static <V> Guarded<V> of(V item, boolean fair)
      Like of(Object), but lets you choose the lock's fairness policy.

      What "fair" means. When several threads are blocked waiting for the lock, a fair lock grants it to the thread that has waited longest — first-in, first-out. A non-fair lock makes no such promise: a fresh caller may barge in and seize the lock the instant it is released, ahead of threads that were already queued. That barging is precisely what makes the non-fair lock faster (it avoids ping-ponging the CPU between threads), which is why it is the default.

      When you would want fair == true. Almost never. Reach for it only when both of the following hold: the lock is under sustained heavy contention (threads are essentially always queued), and you have measured — or must categorically rule out — starvation, where one unlucky thread is repeatedly out-barged and waits far longer than the rest. As a concrete picture: a thread doing short updates in a tight loop can keep barging ahead of a thread that needs just one update to make progress, stalling it indefinitely; a fair lock bounds that wait, paying for it in throughput. Absent a measured problem, leave this false.

      Type Parameters:
      V - the value type
      Parameters:
      item - the initial value; must not be null
      fair - true for a fair (FIFO) lock, false for the higher-throughput default
      Returns:
      a new non-null Guarded
      Throws:
      NullPointerException - if item is null

    • of

      public static <V, U extends V> Guarded<V> of(Class<V> type, U item)
      Creates a non-null Guarded holding item, with an explicit type — useful when you want the container typed as a supertype of the item. Mirrors Var.of(Class, Object).
      Type Parameters:
      V - the declared value type
      U - the (sub)type of the initial item
      Parameters:
      type - the (non-null) declared type of the value
      item - the initial value; must not be null
      Returns:
      a new non-null Guarded of the given type
      Throws:
      NullPointerException - if type or item is null

    • ofNullable

      public static <V> Guarded<@Nullable V> ofNullable(Class<V> type, @Nullable V item)
      Creates a nullable Guarded of the given type, holding item (which may be null). Like Var.ofNullable(Class, Object), this container permits null.
      Type Parameters:
      V - the value type
      Parameters:
      type - the (non-null) declared type of the value
      item - the initial value, possibly null
      Returns:
      a new null-permitting Guarded
      Throws:
      NullPointerException - if type is null
      See Also:
      • ofNull(Class)

    • ofNullable

      public static <V> Guarded<@Nullable V> ofNullable(Class<V> type, @Nullable V item, boolean fair)
      Like ofNullable(Class, Object), but lets you choose the lock's fairness policy. See of(Object, boolean) for an explanation of fairness and when it is worth it.
      Type Parameters:
      V - the value type
      Parameters:
      type - the (non-null) declared type of the value
      item - the initial value, possibly null
      fair - true for a fair (FIFO) lock, false for the higher-throughput default
      Returns:
      a new null-permitting Guarded
      Throws:
      NullPointerException - if type is null

    • ofNull

      public static <V> Guarded<@Nullable V> ofNull(Class<V> type)
      Creates a nullable Guarded of the given type, initially holding null. A concise equivalent of Guarded.ofNullable(type, null), mirroring Var.ofNull(Class).
      Type Parameters:
      V - the value type
      Parameters:
      type - the (non-null) declared type of the value
      Returns:
      a new null-permitting Guarded holding null
      Throws:
      NullPointerException - if type is null

    • type

      public Class<V> type()
      Returns the declared type of the guarded value, like Maybe.type().
      Returns:
      the value's declared Class, never null

    • allowsNull

      public boolean allowsNull()
      Returns whether this container permits null, like Val.allowsNull(). When false, every attempt to store null is rejected at runtime with a NullPointerException, so the container is guaranteed to never hold null.
      Returns:
      true if null is a permitted value

    • get

      public V get()
      Returns the current value.

      Caution: only safe if V is immutable. If V is mutable, the returned reference can be touched outside the lock; use read(Function) to derive what you need while the lock is held instead.

      Returns:
      the current value; null only when this container allowsNull()

    • read

      public <R> R read(Function<? super V,? extends R> reader)
      Applies reader to the current value while holding the lock and returns its result.

      The safe way to look inside a mutable V: compute and return a snapshot (a count, a copy, a boolean) rather than exposing the live object. 

      
 boolean empty = locked.read(List::isEmpty);
      Type Parameters:
      R - the result type
      Parameters:
      reader - a function applied to the value under the lock; must not let the value escape
      Returns:
      the result of reader
      Throws:
      NullPointerException - if reader is null

    • set

      public void set(V newValue)
      Replaces the value.
      Parameters:
      newValue - the new value; may be null only if this container allowsNull()
      Throws:
      NullPointerException - if newValue is null and this container is non-null

    • getAndSet

      public V getAndSet(V newValue)
      Replaces the value and returns the value that was present beforehand.
      Parameters:
      newValue - the new value; may be null only if this container allowsNull()
      Returns:
      the previous value
      Throws:
      NullPointerException - if newValue is null and this container is non-null

    • compareAndSet

      public boolean compareAndSet(V expectedValue, V newValue)
      Sets the value to newValue if the current value equals expectedValue.

      Comparison uses Objects.equals(Object, Object) (value equality), not reference identity as AtomicReference does — which is usually what you want for records and other value types. Because the whole check-and-set happens under the lock, there is no ABA hazard.

      Parameters:
      expectedValue - the value the container must currently hold; may be null only if this container allowsNull()
      newValue - the value to install if the expectation holds; may be null only if this container allowsNull()
      Returns:
      true if the value was updated
      Throws:
      NullPointerException - if newValue is null and this container is non-null

    • update

      public void update(UnaryOperator<V> updater)
      Applies updater to the current value and stores the result. Return value ignored; the readable, fire-and-forget form of updateAndGet(UnaryOperator). 
      
 counter.update(Counter::increment);
      Parameters:
      updater - computes the new value from the current value, run once under the lock
      Throws:
      NullPointerException - if updater is null

    • updateAndGet

      public V updateAndGet(UnaryOperator<V> updater)
      Applies updater to the current value, stores the result, and returns the new value.

      Unlike AtomicReference.updateAndGet, updater is invoked exactly once — it is never retried — so a long or otherwise expensive computation is fine here. The lock is held for the whole call.

      Parameters:
      updater - computes the new value from the current value, run once under the lock
      Returns:
      the new value
      Throws:
      NullPointerException - if updater is null

    • getAndUpdate

      public V getAndUpdate(UnaryOperator<V> updater)
      Applies updater to the current value, stores the result, and returns the previous value. updater is invoked exactly once under the lock.
      Parameters:
      updater - computes the new value from the current value, run once under the lock
      Returns:
      the previous value
      Throws:
      NullPointerException - if updater is null

    • accumulateAndGet

      public V accumulateAndGet(V x, BinaryOperator<V> accumulator)
      Combines the current value with x using accumulator and stores the result, returning the new value. Equivalent to updateAndGet(v -> accumulator.apply(v, x)).

      Mirrors AtomicReference.accumulateAndGet; convenient for "merge this delta into the state": 

      
 totals.accumulateAndGet(todaysSales, Totals::plus);
      Parameters:
      x - the value to combine with the current value
      accumulator - a side-effect-free combining function, run once under the lock
      Returns:
      the new value
      Throws:
      NullPointerException - if accumulator is null

    • getAndAccumulate

      public V getAndAccumulate(V x, BinaryOperator<V> accumulator)
      Combines the current value with x using accumulator and stores the result, returning the previous value.
      Parameters:
      x - the value to combine with the current value
      accumulator - a side-effect-free combining function, run once under the lock
      Returns:
      the previous value
      Throws:
      NullPointerException - if accumulator is null

    • updateIf

      public boolean updateIf(Predicate<? super V> condition, UnaryOperator<V> updater)
      Applies updater only if the current value satisfies condition, atomically with respect to other operations. Both functions run under the lock; updater runs at most once. 
      
 boolean shipped = order.updateIf(Order::isPaid, Order::markShipped);
      Parameters:
      condition - tested against the current value under the lock
      updater - computes the new value, applied only if condition holds
      Returns:
      true if the value was updated
      Throws:
      NullPointerException - if condition or updater is null

    • mutate

      public void mutate(Consumer<? super V> mutator)
      Runs mutator against the current value under the lock, without swapping the reference. The intended door into a deliberately-mutable V (a map, a builder, ...). 
      
 cache.mutate(m -> m.put(key, value));

      Not compatible with viewOn(Executor). A view publishes the value to another thread, which is only safe for immutable V; mutating in place would let that value escape the lock. Use this method only in the guarded-mutable mode, and only when you are not viewing.

      Parameters:
      mutator - a consumer applied to the value under the lock; must not let the value escape
      Throws:
      NullPointerException - if mutator is null

    • tryUpdate

      public boolean tryUpdate(UnaryOperator<V> updater, Duration timeout) throws InterruptedException
      Attempts to acquire the lock within timeout and, if successful, applies updater exactly once and stores the result.

      Use when a caller must not block indefinitely. A non-positive timeout attempts a single immediate acquisition. The wait is interruptible. 

      
 if (!state.tryUpdate(State::advance, Duration.ofMillis(50))) {
     // could not acquire in time — back off, retry, or report busy
 }
      Parameters:
      updater - computes the new value, run once under the lock if it is acquired
      timeout - the maximum time to wait for the lock
      Returns:
      true if the lock was acquired and updater applied; false on timeout (in which case the value is unchanged)
      Throws:
      NullPointerException - if updater or timeout is null
      InterruptedException - if the current thread is interrupted while waiting

    • viewOn

      public Viewable<V> viewOn(Executor executor)
      Returns a read-only Viewable property that mirrors this container's value, with every change delivered through executor. The property — and any Action listeners you register on it — therefore run on the executor's thread, no matter which thread mutated this Guarded. This is the bridge from many-writer guarded state to a single-owner reactive property; see the class documentation for the full rationale.

      The view inherits this container's type and null policy: a non-null Guarded yields a non-null view, a nullable one yields a view that may publish null. A single method therefore covers both — there is no separate nullable variant — and a nullable container can be viewed even while it currently holds null. 

      
 Viewable<Account> view = account.viewOn(uiExecutor);
 view.onChange(From.ALL, it -> render(it.currentValue().orElseThrow()));
 // ...background threads mutate 'account'; 'render' always runs on uiExecutor's thread.

      Contract — read these, misuse fails silently:

      • executor must be effectively single-threaded (e.g. a single-thread executor or a UI dispatch loop). It defines the property's owner thread; a multi-threaded executor would fire the property concurrently and corrupt it.
      • V must be immutable — the value crosses threads, so it must not be mutated afterwards. Do not combine this with mutate(Consumer).
      • Delivery is conflated (latest-wins): if writes outpace executor, intermediate values may be skipped and only the most recent is published.

      The returned view is referenced only weakly by this container: once you drop it, it becomes eligible for garbage collection, stops receiving updates, and its registration is pruned automatically — there is nothing to unsubscribe.

      Parameters:
      executor - the (effectively single-threaded) executor on which the view and its listeners run
      Returns:
      a new read-only Viewable mirroring this container on executor's thread
      Throws:
      NullPointerException - if executor is null

    • viewOn

      public Viewable<V> viewOn(Executor executor, V fallback)
      Like viewOn(Executor), but the resulting view never publishes null: whenever this container holds null, the view shows fallback instead. This is the Guarded analogue of Val.view(nullObject, ..) — the way to turn a nullable shared state into a null-safe reactive property.

      The same contract as viewOn(Executor) applies (effectively single-threaded executor, immutable V, conflated latest-wins delivery, weak retention). The fallback only matters for a nullable container; on a non-null one the value is never null, so it is never used. Note that, because Java cannot express "the non-null version of V", the returned type is still Viewable<V> — but at runtime it is guaranteed to never hold null.

      Parameters:
      executor - the (effectively single-threaded) executor on which the view and its listeners run
      fallback - the non-null value shown whenever this container holds null
      Returns:
      a new read-only Viewable that never publishes null
      Throws:
      NullPointerException - if executor or fallback is null
      IllegalArgumentException - if fallback is not assignable to type()

    • isLocked

      public boolean isLocked()
      Returns whether any thread currently holds the lock. Intended for monitoring and debugging; the result is a momentary snapshot and must not drive control flow.
      Returns:
      true if the lock is held

    • getQueueLength

      public int getQueueLength()
      Returns an estimate of the number of threads waiting to acquire the lock. Intended for monitoring and debugging only.
      Returns:
      the estimated number of waiting threads

    • toString

      public String toString()
      Returns a string form of the current value, read under the lock. Provided for logging and debugging.
      Overrides:
      toString in class Object
      Returns:
      "Guarded[" + value + "]"
